flavor_text: A dented cap from the levy racks, still smelling of forge smoke.
This helm was patched with coffin nails and stubborn hope.
Rust creeps along the brow where rainwater and blood collect.
The visor slit is crooked, yet it has seen men survive.
Hammered thin for recruits, it rings like a cracked bell.
An iron helm issued at dusk before the breach horn.
Soot-black rivets mark repairs made between night assaults.
Its cheek plates were widened after an axe found bone.
The crown bears prayer scratches from a hand that shook.
Forged for gate duty, it turns panic into narrow focus.
Old kill notches line the rim in careful tally marks.
A heavier faceguard meant for fighting in torchless smoke.
The metal keeps the cold like a well at midnight.
Each seam is caulked with pitch from siege ladders.
Lifted from a fallen captain and refitted without ceremony.
The crest slot is empty where a house name was cut away.
Its brow ridge is thick enough to turn glancing steel.
Burn scars lace the visor from oil poured at the walls.
A bastion helm that never fit men who planned retreat.
Forged under storm bells for the king's last gate ward.
The inside leather is dark from years of fevered breath.
It carries a hush like chapels after execution.
Crown etchings map battles no scribe now records.
Only veteran skulls sit easily beneath this weight.
An imperial warhelm made for sieges that outlived dynasties.
A plain cuirass beaten from farm iron and fear.
Its chest plate is thin where the smith spared metal.
Rivets bite through cloth and leave crescent bruises.
The breast seam whistles when arrows pass too close.
Worn over wool, it makes every breath sound guilty.
Field steel layered to stop spearheads at bad angles.
This warcoat was tarred against rain and corpse stench.
A guard harness balanced for shield walls and long marches.
The collar is scarred by blades that slipped past discipline.
Forged ribs spread impact like thunder under stone.
Its inner straps were replaced with reins from dead cavalry.
Dark plate meant for holding stairwells against impossible odds.
The gorget locks with a prayer and a snapped tooth.
Each dent is hammered flat, never polished away.
Worn by keep wardens who slept beside loaded crossbows.
The steel drinks heat until winter feels merciful.
A bastion cuirass that favors endurance over all grace.
Ritual ash is packed beneath the breast ridge for luck.
Its weight teaches the spine to bow only forward.
Forged for siege captains who counted losses aloud.
Blackened plates overlap like coffin lids in a row.
The chest sigil was chiseled off after a failed rebellion.
It turns killing blows into debts paid by bruises.
An oathbound harness carried through three burning capitals.
Imperial plate that feels less worn than inherited doom.
Crude shoulder plates tied with cord and butcher twine.
One pauldron sits lower where an old fracture healed wrong.
Rust flakes gather in the hinge like red snow.
Thin guards that chatter whenever the bearer trembles.
Patched edges chew cloaks and draw small blood.
Reinforced pauldrons made for doorway fights and shield crush.
Their rims are nicked by years of overhead parries.
A militia set widened to catch glancing axe heads.
The left guard bears a saint mark scratched in haste.
Shoulders plated for the first impact and the second.
Each strap was cut from belts taken off deserters.
Heavy spaulders that punish hesitation more than enemy steel.
Scorched lacquer hides where lamp oil once ignited.
Their hinges groan like gates opening to gallows yards.
Worn by vanguard lines that advanced through boiling pitch.
The steel lip throws sparks when blades slide off.
A sentinel pair built for long hours under arrow rain.
Smoke-stained mantlets remembered from the black winter sieges.
These shoulders carry weight like a sentence already passed.
Forged in fortress foundries that never cooled completely.
The right plate is carved with names of the unburied.
Each overlap bites deep, denying hooks and mercy.
A warcrown set reserved for breach leaders and madmen.
Their silhouette is known where city walls once stood.
Paragon pauldrons made for the final push into fire.
Rough gloves stitched from hide over cheap iron scales.
The knuckles are flattened from too many door-break swings.
Cold metal rings bite the fingers at dawn watch.
These mitts leave soot prints on every hilt.
The seams split easily, then mend with wire.
Field gauntlets built to keep grip through wet blood.
Their palms are scarred smooth by pike shafts.
A guard pair weighted for shield bashes and hooks.
Finger plates rattle like teeth in winter marches.
Forged wrists to steady the hand after hard impacts.
The leather reeks of oil, rain, and old fear.
Warhands that turn parries into cracked bones.
Burn marks on the cuffs speak of lantern fights.
Each knuckle ridge is filed for close work.
Worn by tower wardens who never fought fair.
The gauntlet locks stiffen when panic would shake aim.
Gripguards tempered to hold steel through tendon pain.
Sooted joints whisper when the fist closes.
These fists were made to end arguments quickly.
Forged for execution details in riot years.
Inner rings catch prayer beads and snap them.
Their weight keeps hands low and killing lines honest.
A dreadforged pair with edges keen as spite.
Veterans say they feel warm before ambush.
Paragon gauntlets that close like gates on a throat.
A plain belt of cracked leather and riveted scrap.
Its buckle is bent from hauling too much steel.
The strap smells of tallow, mud, and old smoke.
Thin links jingle like coins in a pauper's fist.
Stitched patches mark where blades once cut through.
A warbelt that keeps plates from drifting in sprint.
Its hook rings are sized for grenades and gut knives.
Guard girdle weighted to anchor a shielded stance.
The clasp bears tooth marks from a battlefield repair.
Forged buckle holds when panic twists the spine.
Each notch was cut after a campaign survived.
A steel cincture worn by men who dug their own trenches.
Oil-black straps hide blood that never washed out.
The waistguard bites hard when the charge begins.
Used by breach crews carrying ladders through fire.
Its chain fringe rattles like distant bones.
A bastion belt that steadies the core under impact.
Sigil pins were removed, leaving pale scars in leather.
The buckle face shows a crown split down the middle.
Forged for captains who marched with no rear line.
This girdle sat on a throne guard found faceless.
Every ring is closed, every weakness anticipated.
A warcrown cincture for duels fought on broken stairs.
It clamps the waist like judgment.
Paragon belt cast for emperors who died standing.
Cheap legguards strapped over patched wool trousers.
The knee plates wobble but still turn shallow cuts.
Rust stains run downward like dried river blood.
Thin faulds clatter through every hurried step.
The hip ties were replaced with braided horsehair.
Field tassets fitted for march mud and spear rush.
Their edges are rolled to spare the inner thigh.
A guard set that favors forward lunges over retreat.
Old dents nest where crossbow bolts struck low.
Forged legplates meant for stair fights and crush lines.
The greave straps creak like wet ropes.
War faulds that keep moving when the body begs rest.
Scored steel traces each narrow escape.
Their weight punishes sloppy footwork immediately.
Worn by wall troops who held until relief never came.
The knee cops are thick enough to kneel on rubble.
A sentinel lower harness for relentless advance.
Ash packed in seams dulls the clang of each step.
These plates remember every ladder climbed under fire.
Forged in famine years when iron cost less than men.
The thighs are etched with vows to nameless dead.
Each hinge moves smooth as a drawn verdict.
A dreadforged leg set for storming murder holes.
Veterans pass them down with funeral instructions.
Paragon legplates built for wars that erase maps.
Crude boots capped with thin iron and river grit.
The soles are nailed twice where cobbles split them.
Mud dries in the seams like graveyard clay.
Worn treads that still bite when panic turns to sprint.
The toe caps are dented from kicking barred doors.
Field warboots made for ditch water and broken stone.
Greave-linked straps keep ankles from folding under load.
A guard pair that plants hard on shield impact.
Old blood darkens the stitching around the heel.
Forged sabatons that ring like chapel bells on stairs.
Their weight drags, then teaches steadier steps.
Marchplates worn by men who crossed arrow fields at dusk.
Scorched leather curls where pitch once burned.
The heel spikes were filed for silent corridors.
Used in breach tunnels where air tasted of lime and rot.
Steel-shod boots that hold ground against charging mass.
A sentinel pair balanced for long siege watches.
Their soles leave sharp prints in ash and sleet.
These treads carry the rhythm of a hanging drum.
Forged for fortress storms and midnight countercharges.
The tongue leather is stamped with an obsolete crest.
Each step lands like a mailed promise.
A dreadforged pair for climbing walls under fire.
Veterans call them coffin boots without irony.
Paragon sabatons made to walk through fallen thrones.
